grammarLessons.name: Articles — le, la, les, un, une
Les Pronoms COD — Direct Object Pronouns

cards.grammarNote:
**bonjour** = **bon** (good) + **jour** (day), from Latin **bonus dies**.
Used for both 'good morning' and 'good afternoon'. In France, saying bonjour when entering any shop or meeting anyone is culturally essential — skipping it reads as rude.

**s'il vous plaît** = 'if it pleases you' (formal/plural). Informal: **s'il te plaît**.
Breaking it down: **s'il** = si + il (if it) · **vous** = you (formal) · **plaît** = pleases (from **plaire**).
Can open a request: S'il vous plaît, où est… ?

**merci** comes from Latin **mercedem** (payment, reward) — the same root as English 'mercy'.
**Merci beaucoup** = thank you very much · **merci bien** = thanks a lot (can be slightly sarcastic in tone).
Response: **de rien** (you're welcome) or **avec plaisir** (with pleasure, more warm).

**vouloir** is irregular: je **veux**, tu **veux**, il **veut**, nous **voulons**, vous **voulez**, ils **veulent**.
In a shop or restaurant, **je voudrais** (I would like) is more polite than **je veux** — the conditional softens the request.

**avoir besoin de** = to need (literally 'to have need of').
j'ai besoin d'aide · j'ai besoin de temps
**de** contracts before a vowel: besoin **d'**aide.

**je m'appelle** uses the reflexive verb **s'appeler** — literally 'I call myself'. Much more natural than 'mon nom est'.
The double l in *appelle* preserves the /ɛl/ sound.

**je** becomes **j'** before a vowel or silent h: **j'**aime, **j'**habite, **j'**ai.
This elision is mandatory — *je aime* is never written or spoken.
Subject pronouns in French cannot be dropped as in Spanish; **je** must always appear.

**tu** = informal (one person you know) · **vous** = formal or plural.
Using tu with a stranger can feel rude; vous is always safe. The shift from vous to tu (**tutoyer**) is a social milestone in French relationships.

**il** = he (for a person) or 'it' (for any masculine noun).
**elle** = she, or 'it' for feminine nouns.
Every French noun has grammatical gender — there is no neutral pronoun. **le livre** → il est intéressant. **la table** → elle est grande.

**elle** = she (person) or 'it' for any feminine noun.
Plural: **elles** (all-female group) · **ils** (mixed or all-male group).
Note: a group of 100 women + 1 man = **ils** in traditional grammar — though modern usage is evolving.

**bonne nuit** = **bonne** (good, fem.) + **nuit** (night, fem.) — **la nuit** is feminine.
Only used when someone is going to bed — not as a general evening farewell.
For a daytime farewell: **bonne journée** · evening departure: **bonne soirée**.

**comment allez-vous ?** = formal inversion form (**allez-vous** = are you going).
Informal: **comment tu vas ?** or just **ça va ?**
**Comment ça va ?** literally means 'how is it going?' — a very common casual greeting.
The standard reply: **ça va bien, merci** (going well, thanks).
cards.englishPhrase: How are you? Very well, thanks.

cards.grammarNote:
**enchanté(e)** = past participle of **enchanter** (to enchant) — literally 'enchanted'.
Adjectives agree with the speaker's gender: a man says **enchanté**, a woman says **enchantée**.
**Enchanté de faire votre connaissance** is the full, formal phrase.

**de rien** = literally 'of nothing' — the most common informal response to merci.
More warm alternatives: **avec plaisir** (with pleasure) · **je vous en prie** (formal, 'I beg of you') · **c'est normal** (it's nothing).
In Québec French, **bienvenue** is used for 'you're welcome'.

French has no neutral pronoun — every noun is masculine (**il**) or feminine (**elle**).
For abstract ideas or pointing at things: **ça** (that/it). C'est ça = 'that's it / exactly'.

**on** is used conversationally to mean 'we' — more common than **nous** in everyday speech.
on takes the same verb form as il/elle: on mange, on va, on est.

**ils** = masculine or mixed-gender group · **elles** = all-feminine group.
Liaison: **ils_** and **elles_** link to a following vowel — **ils_ont**, **elles_arrivent**.
In spoken French, the subject pronoun is always required unlike in languages like Spanish or Italian.

**ce** (m.) / **cet** (m. before vowel) / **cette** (f.) + noun:
ce livre · cet homme · cette table
As a standalone pronoun: **ceci** (this) · **cela/ça** (that).

French uses the same demonstrative adjectives for 'this' and 'that': **ce/cet/cette/ces**.
To distinguish this vs. that, add **-ci** (here) or **-là** (there): ce livre**-ci** (this book) vs. ce livre**-là** (that book).
**Cette** is feminine: **cette** personne, **cette** table.

Possessives agree with the thing possessed, not the owner:
**mon** livre (m.) · **ma** table (f.) · **mes** livres (pl.)
Before a feminine noun starting with a vowel: **mon** amie (not *ma amie*).

**qui** = who (subject) in both questions and relative clauses.
**Qui est cette personne ?** (question) · **la personne qui parle** (relative clause)
As an object: **que** — **la personne que je connais** (the person I know).

**quoi** = what (standalone or after a preposition): de quoi, avec quoi, pour quoi.
In questions: **qu'est-ce que** (what, as object) is the most common spoken form.
**Que** is used in formal inversion: **Que voulez-vous ?**
Never say *quoi est-ce* — use qu'est-ce que or quoi at the end: Tu veux quoi ?

**quand** = when (question word or conjunction).
As a question: **Quand est-ce que tu arrives ?** or informal **Tu arrives quand ?**
As a conjunction: **quand il pleut** (when it rains) — note that French uses present or future tense after quand for future meaning, just like English.

**où** = where (always with an accent grave to distinguish from the conjunction **ou** = or).
**Où sont les toilettes ?** (formal) · **Les toilettes, c'est où ?** (casual)
Also used in relative clauses: **la ville où j'habite** (the city where I live).

**pourquoi** = **pour** (for) + **quoi** (what) — literally 'for what?'
The answer uses **parce que** (because): **Pourquoi ? Parce que…**
Don't confuse with **car** (for, because) which is more literary.
cards.phrase: Pourquoi tu ne peux pas venir ?

cards.grammarNote:
**comment** = how. Also used for 'what did you say?' (politely): **Comment ?**
**Comment tu vas ?** (informal) · **Comment allez-vous ?** (formal inversion)
**Comment dit-on… ?** = how do you say…? — a useful learner's phrase.

**bonjour** covers both 'good morning' and 'good afternoon'. **bonsoir** = good evening (from ~6pm). **bonne nuit** = good night (only when going to bed).
In France, saying bonjour when entering a shop is expected.
cards.englishPhrase: Good morning! Did you sleep well?

cards.grammarNote:
**chercher** = to look for (a regular -er verb: je cherche, tu cherches…).
Importantly, **chercher** already includes 'for' — never say *chercher pour*.
Compare: **trouver** (to find) · **retrouver** (to find again / to meet up with).

**pardon** = excuse me (passing someone or mishearing).
**excusez-moi** = excuse me (getting attention, more formal).
**s'il vous plaît** can also open a request: 'S'il vous plaît, où est…?'
cards.englishPhrase: Excuse me, where is the main street?
cards.phrase: Pardon, où est la rue principale ?

cards.grammarNote:
**pouvoir** (to be able to / can) is irregular: je **peux**, tu **peux**, il **peut**, nous **pouvons**, vous **pouvez**, ils **peuvent**.
**Vous pouvez m'aider ?** = informal question (rising intonation)
Formal equivalent: **Pouvez-vous m'aider ?** (inversion).

**ne…pas** wraps the verb in negation: je **ne** sais **pas**.
In spoken French, **ne** is very commonly dropped: **Je sais pas où c'est.**
**Savoir** (to know a fact) vs. **connaître** (to know a person or place) — two different verbs for 'to know'.
cards.englishPhrase: I don't know where it is.
cards.phrase: Je ne sais pas où c'est.

cards.grammarNote:
**avoir** is used for physical states (not être):
j'ai faim · j'ai soif · j'ai froid/chaud · j'ai peur
These are 'having' a sensation, not 'being' one.
cards.englishPhrase: I'm hungry. Do you have water?
cards.phrase: J'ai faim. Tu as de l'eau ?

cards.grammarNote:
**faire** = to do AND to make — one verb covers both English meanings.
Irregular: je **fais**, tu **fais**, il **fait**, nous **faisons**, vous **faites**, ils **font**.
Also used for weather: **il fait chaud** (it's hot), **il fait beau** (it's nice out).

**manger** is a regular -er verb but adds an **e** before **-ons** to keep the soft g sound: nous mang**e**ons.
With partitive articles for food: **je mange du pain**, **je mange de la salade**, **je mange des fruits**.
After negation the partitive becomes **de**: je **ne** mange **pas de** viande.

**aimer** = to love (people) or to like (things).
**aimer bien** softens it to 'like' rather than 'love': J'aime bien le café. Saying j'aime le café is fine but stronger.

**mais** = but. One of the MAISOB conjunctions (mais, alors, if, so, or, but — a classic mnemonic is **MAISOB**: **m**ais, **a**lors, **i**l, **s**i, **o**u, **b**ien).
Note: **mais** vs. **mais non** vs. **mais si** — **mais si** is used to contradict a negative: 'Tu n'aimes pas ça ?' → 'Mais si!' (Yes I do!).
cards.englishPhrase: I want to go, but I can't.
cards.phrase: Je veux y aller, mais je ne peux pas.

cards.grammarNote:
**et** = and. The t is never pronounced before a vowel — no liaison: **et_** + vowel does NOT link.
This is a key exception to liaison rules: 'un café **et** un thé' — the t is silent.
Note the partitive articles in the phrase: **de l'**eau (de + l') and **de la** nourriture.
cards.phrase: De l'eau et de la nourriture.

cards.grammarNote:
**ou** (or) must be distinguished from **où** (where) — the accent marks a completely different word.
**ou bien** = or else / or rather (slightly more emphatic).
In questions: **Thé ou café ?** — rising intonation makes it a question without any question word.

French uses **avoir** for hunger, thirst, and temperature — never être:
**j'ai faim** (hungry) · **j'ai soif** (thirsty) · **j'ai froid/chaud** (cold/hot)

**soif** is a feminine noun (la soif) though you rarely see it with an article in this phrase.
**J'ai très soif** — note that **très** modifies the noun here, not an adjective, which is unusual in English ('I'm very thirsty') but natural in French.
Related: **mourir de soif** (dying of thirst — a common hyperbolic expression).

**la personne** = person (always feminine, even when referring to a man).
Double duty: **personne** also means 'nobody' in negation: **je ne vois personne** (I see nobody).
Context distinguishes: **une personne** (a person) vs. **ne…personne** (nobody).

**un endroit** = a place, spot, location (masculine).
**un lieu** is a more formal synonym: un lieu de rencontre (a meeting place).
**une place** = a square (public space) or a seat — not a generic 'place': **la place de la Bastille**, **une place dans le train**.
cards.englishPhrase: Do you know a good place?

cards.grammarNote:
**une chose** = a thing (feminine).
**quelque chose** = something (invariable): **tu veux quelque chose ?**
**quelque chose de bon** — after **quelque chose de**, the adjective is always masculine: **quelque chose de délicieux**, **quelque chose d'intéressant**.

**ici** = here (location) · **là** = there, but also used for 'here' in spoken French.
**Viens ici !** = Come here! (using **tu** form of **venir**: viens).
**par ici** = this way · **par là** = that way — useful for giving directions.

**là** = there, but also frequently used to mean 'here' in casual speech: **je suis là** (I'm here).
**là-bas** = over there (more distant) · **là-haut** = up there · **là-dedans** = in there.
Note: **là** (there/here) vs. **la** (the, feminine article) — the accent makes all the difference.

**très** = very (modifies adjectives and some adverbs): **très bien**, **très grand**, **très lentement**.
Don't confuse with **trop** (too/too much): **trop chaud** (too hot) vs. **très chaud** (very hot).
Note: **très** cannot directly modify a noun — use **beaucoup** instead: **beaucoup de travail** (a lot of work).

**au revoir** = **au** (à + le) + **revoir** (to see again) — literally 'until we see each other again'.
Informal: **salut** (also used for hello), **ciao**, **à plus** (see ya), **à tout à l'heure** (see you soon).
**À bientôt** = see you soon · **À demain** = see you tomorrow · **À samedi** = see you Saturday.

**eau** is feminine. Use the partitive **de l'eau** for an unspecified amount.
The partitive (**du** / **de la** / **de l'**) is used when quantity is unspecified — very common with food and drink.
cards.phrase: Un verre d'eau, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**la nourriture** = food in general (uncountable, feminine).
Alternatives: **les aliments** (foodstuffs, more technical) · **à manger** (something to eat, informal): **j'ai acheté à manger**.
Use the partitive for an unspecified amount: **de la nourriture** (some food).

**la maison** = house or home (feminine). From Latin **mansio**.
**chez moi** = at my place/home — much more natural than 'à ma maison' in everyday speech: **je rentre chez moi** (I'm going home).
**à la maison** = at home (when staying in) · **à la maison** vs. **chez moi** are largely interchangeable.

**le magasin** = store, shop (general).
**une boutique** = a small, often fashionable shop.
**au magasin** — à + le = **au**: je vais **au** magasin.
Specific shops: **la boulangerie** (bakery), **la pharmacie** (pharmacy), **l'épicerie** (grocery).

**les espèces** = cash (always plural in this sense).
**en espèces** = in cash · **par carte** = by card.
Note the formal register of **Vous payez** (are you paying?) — using **vous** to address a customer is standard in shops.
cards.phrase: Vous payez en espèces ou par carte ?

cards.grammarNote:
**l'aide** is feminine: **une aide** (a help/an aid).
**aider** = to help (regular -er verb): **je vous aide ?** (can I help you?)
**Au secours !** = Help! (emergency) · **à l'aide !** = Help! (less urgent, calling for assistance).

**les toilettes** = the toilet/restroom (almost always plural in French).
**la salle de bain** = the bathroom (where you bathe/shower) — the two rooms are often separate in French homes.
In public: **les WC** (pronounced 'veh-say') is also common. Never say **la bathroom** — it's an anglicism.

**toujours** = always OR still (two meanings depending on context).
**Je prends toujours un café** = I always have a coffee.
**Il est toujours là** = He's still there.
**Toujours** comes after the verb in simple tenses and between **avoir/être** and the past participle: **j'ai toujours aimé le café**.
cards.englishPhrase: I always drink coffee in the morning.
cards.phrase: Je prends toujours un café le matin.

cards.grammarNote:
**jamais** pairs with **ne** to form the negative: ne … jamais.
In spoken French the ne is often dropped: J'arrive jamais en retard.
**Jamais** alone in answer to a question means 'never'.

**aller + infinitive** = the near future (futur proche):
je vais manger · on va partir · tu vas voir
The most common way to talk about immediate future plans in French.

**venir** is irregular: je **viens**, tu **viens**, il **vient**, nous **venons**, vous **venez**, ils **viennent**.
**venir de + infinitive** = to have just done something (recent past): **je viens de manger** (I just ate).
**Tu viens d'où ?** = Where are you from? — literally 'you come from where?'

**comprendre** (to understand) is conjugated like **prendre**: je **comprends**, tu **comprends**, il **comprend**, nous **comprenons**.
**Je ne comprends pas** — full negation with **ne…pas**.
**Vous comprenez ?** (Do you understand?) uses **vous** for formal/plural address.

**parler** is a regular -er verb: je **parle**, tu **parles**, il **parle**, nous **parlons**, vous **parlez**, ils **parlent**.
Languages follow **parler** directly with no article: **parler français**, **parler anglais** — not *parler le français*.
Note **vous** here — addressing a stranger formally.

**donner** is a regular -er verb.
**Donnez-moi** = give me (imperative + indirect object pronoun **moi**).
Word order with pronouns changes in imperatives: in statements **me** comes before the verb, but in positive imperatives **moi** follows: **donnez-**moi, **donne-**moi.
cards.phrase: Donnez-moi le menu, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**voir** is irregular: je **vois**, tu **vois**, il **voit**, nous **voyons**, vous **voyez**, ils **voient**.
**Je ne peux rien voir** — double object: **ne…rien** (nothing) wraps the verb plus infinitive.
**rien** in negation replaces a direct object: je ne vois **rien** (I see nothing) vs. je ne vois **pas** de… (I don't see any…).

**avec** = with. Preposition, always followed by a noun or pronoun.
**Je suis venu** uses **être** in the passé composé because **venir** is a movement verb — past participle **venu** agrees with the subject (masculine singular here).
A woman would say: **je suis venue** avec mon ami.
cards.phrase: Je suis venu avec mon ami.

cards.grammarNote:
**sans** = without. Always followed by a noun with **no article**: **sans sucre**, **sans lait**, **sans problème**.
With an indefinite article, the article is dropped after **sans**: *café sans un sucre* → **café sans sucre**.
Contrast: **avec** (with) keeps the article: **avec du lait**.
cards.phrase: Café sans sucre, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**pour** = for (purpose, destination, duration in future).
**C'est pour toi** — **toi** is the stressed/disjunctive pronoun (moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles).
Used after prepositions: **pour moi**, **avec toi**, **sans lui**.

**dans** = inside a space (dans le café) · **sur** = on top of (sur la table)
**en** = in + language/country/material: en français, en France, en coton
**à** = at/to a specific place: à Paris, au magasin
cards.englishPhrase: The book is on the table.
cards.phrase: Le livre est sur la table.

cards.grammarNote:
**de** = from, of, about.
**Je suis de Paris** = I'm from Paris.
**de** contracts with **le** → **du** and with **les** → **des**: **du magasin**, **des enfants**.
Before a vowel: **de l'** — **de l'**école, **de l'**eau.

**bon** (m.) / **bonne** (f.) — agrees with the noun it modifies.
**C'est une bonne idée** — **idée** is feminine, so **bonne**.
**Bien** is also 'good' but as an adverb: **c'est bien** (that's good/nice) vs. **c'est bon** (that tastes good / it's good).

**mauvais** (m.) / **mauvaise** (f.) — adjective agrees with its noun.
**C'est une mauvaise nouvelle** — **nouvelle** (news/a piece of news) is feminine.
Adjective placement: **mauvais** usually precedes the noun: **une mauvaise journée**, **un mauvais signe**.

**grand** (m.) / **grande** (f.) — precedes the noun (BAGS adjective: Beauty, Age, Goodness, Size).
**Un grand café** = a large coffee (or a prestigious café — context matters!).
**Grand** can also mean 'great' or 'tall': **un grand homme** (a great man) vs. **un homme grand** (a tall man). Position changes meaning!
cards.phrase: Un grand café, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**petit** (m.) / **petite** (f.) — precedes the noun, like **grand**.
**Une petite table** — **table** is feminine, so **petite**.
**Petit** also carries affectionate meaning: **mon petit** (my little one/dear). **Petit-déjeuner** = breakfast (literally 'small lunch').
cards.phrase: Une petite table, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**beaucoup** = a lot, much, many.
**beaucoup de** + noun (no article): **beaucoup de travail**, **beaucoup d'eau**, **beaucoup de gens**.
In negation, keep **pas**: **je ne travaille pas beaucoup** (I don't work much).
**Beaucoup** cannot directly modify an adjective — use **très** for that: **très grand**, not *beaucoup grand*.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a lot of work.

cards.grammarNote:
**un peu** = a little. **un peu de** + noun (no article): **un peu d'eau**, **un peu de temps**.
**un peu** can soften adjectives: **c'est un peu difficile** (it's a little difficult).
Contrast: **peu de** (without **un**) = little/not much — more negative in tone: **j'ai peu de temps** (I have little time).
cards.phrase: Un peu d'eau, s'il vous plaît.

cards.grammarNote:
**Comment dit-on… ?** uses **on** as an impersonal pronoun ('one' / 'you' in general).
Inversion: **dit-on** — the verb comes before the pronoun (formal question structure).
Alternative: **Comment on dit… ?** (informal, no inversion) is more common in speech.
cards.englishPhrase: How do you say 'thank you' in French?

cards.grammarNote:
**Pouvez-vous** is formal inversion — subject **vous** moves after the verb **pouvez**.
**m'aider** = **me** + **aider** with elision before a vowel: **m'**.
Informal alternative: **Tu peux m'aider ?** (rising intonation, no inversion needed).

**C'est combien ?** = casual, very common.
**Ça coûte combien ?** = more explicit (how much does it cost?).
**Combien ça fait ?** = how much does that come to? (at a till/register).
**Quel est le prix ?** = what is the price? (more formal).

**désolé** = past participle of **désoler** (to distress), used as an adjective meaning 'sorry'.
Gender agreement: **désolé** (m.) / **désolée** (f.) — a woman says **je suis désolée**.
**Je suis vraiment désolé** = I'm really sorry. **Pardon** is used for smaller apologies or interruptions.

**pas de problème** drops the **ne** entirely — a very common ellipsis in French.
Full form would be: **il n'y a pas de problème** (there's no problem).
**ne t'inquiète pas** = don't worry (reflexive imperative of **s'inquiéter**) — uses **tu** form, so this phrase is informal.
cards.phrase: Pas de problème, ne t'inquiète pas.
